content.mi:
Te Raorao o Ōtara

I te raorao mārie o Ōtara, i reira te kohu e takai ana pēnei i te korowai ki ngā puke, ka noho tētahi taniwha ko Te Tahi-o-Te-Rā.

Ko tōna kāinga he ana huna i raro i ngā wai kanapa, e mōhiotia ana e te iwi ko Te Ana-o-Te Tahi.

I rata ngā tamariki o te raorao ki te kaukau i te taha o taua ana — engari i te wā mārama anake te wai.

I mōhio te katoa: ka tīaho te wai pēnei i te kōura, kua hoki mai a Te Tahi, ā, me waiho te awa kia tau te rangimārie.
content.en:
The Valley of Ōtara

In the quiet valley of Ōtara, where mist curls like soft korowai around the hills, there lived a taniwha named Te Tahi-o-Te-Rā.

His home was a hidden cave beneath the shimmering waters, a place known to the people as Te Ana-o-Te Tahi.

The children of the valley loved to swim near the cave — but only when the water was clear.

For everyone knew: when the water glowed gold like kōura, Te Tahi had returned, and it was time to leave the river in peace.
